blocks.title: Learning objectives
blocks.content: Predict polynomial end behaviour, connect factors to zeros, and describe rational-function restrictions and asymptotes.

blocks.title: Why this matters
blocks.content: Graphs let you test whether an algebraic model makes sense before calculating every point. This is useful whenever a relationship has turning points, restrictions, or long-term behaviour.

blocks.title: End behaviour
blocks.content: End behaviour describes what happens to f(x) as x becomes very large positive or negative. For a polynomial, its leading term controls this behaviour.

blocks.title: Four leading-term patterns
blocks.content: Sketch the four cases: even degree positive, even degree negative, odd degree positive, and odd degree negative. Label both ends of each graph.

blocks.title: Worked example 1: polynomial zeros
blocks.content: For f(x)=(x-2)(x+1)^2, the zeros are x=2 and x=-1. The graph crosses at x=2 and touches/turns at x=-1 because that factor has even multiplicity.

blocks.title: Worked example 2: rational restrictions
blocks.content: For r(x)=(x+3)/(x-2), x=2 is excluded from the domain and creates a vertical asymptote. Because numerator and denominator have equal degree, y=1 is the horizontal asymptote.

blocks.title: A hole is not always an asymptote
blocks.content: If a common factor cancels, its excluded x-value creates a hole. A factor left in the denominator creates a vertical asymptote.

blocks.title: Domain first
blocks.content: State restrictions before simplifying a rational expression. Cancelling a factor does not restore an excluded input.

blocks.title: Quick check and practice
blocks.content:
1. State the end behaviour of -2x^5+x.
2. Find the zeros and multiplicities of (x-4)^2(x+2).
3. State the domain restrictions and asymptotes of (x-1)/(x^2-9).

blocks.title: Solutions
blocks.content:
1. Left end up, right end down.
2. x=4 has multiplicity 2; x=-2 has multiplicity 1.
3. x≠-3,3; vertical asymptotes x=-3 and x=3; horizontal asymptote y=0.

cards.front: What does an even multiplicity zero do?
cards.back: The graph touches the x-axis and turns around.
A denominator factor that remains after simplification.

questions.question: Which input is excluded from f(x)=1/(x+5)?
What happens at a zero with multiplicity 2?
questions.explanation: Even multiplicity causes a touch/turn behaviour.

questions.question: Explain the difference between a hole and a vertical asymptote.
questions.sample_answer: A strong answer explains that both come from excluded values, but a hole comes from a cancelled common factor while a vertical asymptote remains because the denominator is zero after simplification.

blocks.title: Before the next lesson
blocks.content: Use factors to locate zeros, then use multiplicity and the leading term to predict graph shape. For rational functions, list domain restrictions before simplifying.

blocks.title: Study tip
blocks.content: Draw a five-second rough sketch before using a graphing tool. Then use the tool to check your reasoning, not replace it.
